claim: Bond-market reporting in late 2025 and early 2026 has highlighted a recurring pattern in private-equity-backed data-center financings: investment-grade ratings paired with yields that historically sit in the speculative-grade (high-yield, or 'junk') range. Specifically, the Corporate Gossip podcast cites a Morningstar/S&P-rated bond issued to fund a private-equity data-center investment, assigned an A+ rating but trading at a 6.58 percent yield — a yield range typically associated with non-investment-grade paper. The historical parallel cited by the podcast is the structuring of mortgage-backed securities in the run-up to 2008, where investment-grade tranches contained underlying loans of varying credit quality.

sources.label: S&P Global Market Intelligence — “Private equity investment surge sends US data center deals to 5-year high”
Bank for International Settlements — “Financing the AI boom: from cash flows to debt”
Wikipedia — Bond credit rating (reference for the A+ rating tier and the conventional yield/rating relationship)
Corporate Gossip podcast, Instagram reel DSfVRVcgHLl (Dec 20, 2025) — surfacing source

notes: The specific bond cited (A+ rating, 6.58 percent yield) is referenced by the podcast hosts as appearing in Morningstar/S&P-published bond data; the exact issuer/issuance has not been named in this record and is left as a pending primary citation. The macro pattern (rapid private-equity expansion into data-center deals, growing reliance on private credit) is well-documented in the linked S&P Global and BIS sources.
